scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Plato's Symposium is set in Athens and describes the social conventions (nomos) of the Athenian elite during the late 5th century BCE.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the approximate time of the composition of the Symposium (c. 385 BCE), reflecting contemporary Athenian pederastic norms described by Pausanias in the text.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text links character traits to success in 'public careers' (ta politika), specifically referring to the Athenian system of citizen magistrate eligibility.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Plato's floruit and the period of the dialogue's composition, reflecting the Athenian democratic reality of the 4th century BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Agathon explicitly cites 'our city's sovereign, the law,' referring to the legal framework of Athens regarding voluntary contracts (homologia).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Plato reflects the contemporary Athenian legal principle that voluntary agreements are binding, a cornerstone of 4th-century BCE Athenian law.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'stress of custom' refers to the social and religious obligations of Athenian citizens to maintain the oikos (household) through marriage and procreation.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the social pressures and customs of 4th-century BCE Athens regarding the duty of citizens to marry.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The philosophical categorization of knowledge versus opinion reflects the intellectual environment of Plato's Academy in Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the composition of the Symposium, where these epistemological distinctions were central to Platonic thought.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The description of 'spiritual men' vs 'mechanicals' reflects the social hierarchy and religious specialists (manteis) found in classical Greek poleis like Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the contemporary Athenian distinction between religious expertise and manual labor during Plato's lifetime.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The exclusion of the 'banausos' (artisan/manual laborer) from certain types of spiritual or intellectual status was a standard feature of Athenian elite ideology.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects 4th-century BCE Athenian elite views on the social and spiritual limitations of those engaged in manual trades.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The biological and social milestones discussed in the text correspond to the life stages (maturity) recognized in Athenian social practice.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the general Greek and specifically Athenian understanding of the onset of reproductive age as a social and physical transition.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text references both Spartan (Lycurgus) and Athenian (Solon) lawgivers, as well as the wider Greek custom of honoring such figures with cult status.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Anchored to the era of Solon's reforms in Athens (-594), one of the primary examples used to illustrate the rule.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'rites and revelations' described are a philosophical adaptation of the mystery religions prominent in Athens (like the Eleusinian Mysteries).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the composition of the Symposium, reflecting the developmental educational model Plato proposed for the Athenian elite.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'right method of boy-loving' refers to the institutionalized pederastic relationship (paideia) characteristic of the Athenian elite.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the pedagogical and social norms of elite Athens during Plato's lifetime.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The setting is a private home in Athens, following the social protocols of the Athenian symposium.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The dramatic date of the Symposium is 416 BCE, the year Agathon won the Lenaea.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The presence of a paidagogos (attendant) for a youth is a well-documented feature of Athenian social life for the wealthy.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the social reality of the late Peloponnesian War era in Athens, the setting for Alcibiades' anecdote.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The use of 'boys' (paides) as domestic servants at symposia is a ubiquitous feature of classical Athenian slavery.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Standard practice in the late 5th century Athens; the dramatic year of the banquet is 416 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The exclusion of 'profane' servants from the esoteric conversation of the elite reflects the social and intellectual boundaries of the Athenian polis.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the privacy norms of elite Athenian social gatherings at the time of the dramatic setting.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Alcibiades was a member of the Athenian aristocracy; his ability to leverage property reflects the rights of his class in Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the property rights and social influence of the Athenian wealthy at the height of Alcibiades' career.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'prize for valor' (aristeia) and the influence of generals (strategoi) were central parts of the Athenian military and political honor system.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Alcibiades refers to the Battle of Potidaea (-432), where these military honors were actually contested.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Meno, a Thessalian, and Socrates discuss 'virtue' (arete) in a manner that generalizes the expected role of male citizens across Greek poleis.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dramatic date of the Meno is approximately 402 BCE, just before Meno's departure for the expedition with Cyrus.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The definition of a woman's duty as managing the interior of the house was a pan-Hellenic norm of the classical period.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the contemporary social gender roles in Greece during the lifetime of the dialogue's participants.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The ontological exclusion of slaves from political governance was a defining characteristic of the Greek slave-holding polis.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the universal Greek distinction between the status of 'slave' and the act of 'governing' circa 402 BCE.
